elements.sent-2411-001: This paper explores rotation estimation from the perspective of special unitary matrices.
elements.sent-2411-002: First, multiple solutions to Wahba's problem are derived through special unitary matrices, providing linear constraints on quaternion rotation parameters.
elements.sent-2411-003: Next, from these constraints, closed-form solutions to the problem are presented for minimal cases.
elements.sent-2411-004: Finally, motivated by these results, we investigate new representations for learning rotations in neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-005: Numerous experiments validate the proposed methods.
elements.sent-2411-006: Many representations have been developed over the years to describe them including rotation matrices, Euler angles, and quaternions.
elements.sent-2411-007: Each method has specific strengths such as parameter efficiency, singularity avoidance, or interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-008: While special orthogonal matrices $SO(3)$ are widely used, their complex counterparts, special unitary matrices $SU(2)$, are less explored in areas like robotics and computer vision.
elements.sent-2411-009: This paper showcases the utility of special unitary matrices by tackling rotation estimation from different perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-010: Wahba's problem is a fundamental problem in attitude estimation introduced in 1965 by its namesake Grace Wahba~\cite{Wahba .}
elements.sent-2411-011: The task refers to the process of determining the orientation of a target coordinate frame relative to a reference coordinate frame based on 3D unit vector observations.
elements.sent-2411-012: More formally, it is phrased as seeking the optimal rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}$ that minimizes the following loss: \begin{equation}\min_{R \in SO(3)} \sum_{i} w_{i}||b_{i} - Ra_{i}||^{2} \label{eq:prob}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{a _{i}$ are the observations in the reference frame, $\mathbf{b _{i}$ are the corresponding observations in the target frame, and $w_{i}$ are the real positive weights for each observation pair.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Wahba showed that this problem can be solved analytically by finding the nearest special orthogonal matrix (in a Frobenius sense) to the matrix $\mathbf{B}$ as follows: \begin{equation}B = \sum_{i} w_{i}b_{i}a_{i}^{T} \label{eq:B} R = (B^{T})^{-1}(B^{T}B)^{\frac{1}{2}} \label{eq:wahba_sol}\end{equation} Today, the result is typically computed via singular value decomposition~\cite{Wahba_SVD .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Davenport introduced the first such method in 1968~\cite{Davenport by showing that the corresponding optimal quaternion $\mathbf{q}$ is the eigenvector corresponding to the largest eigenvalue of the 4x4 symmetric gain matrix $\mathbf{K}$ (see~\cite{Wahba_overview for construction).}}
elements.sent-2411-015: The solution via eigendecomposition is relatively slow as it solves for all of the eigenvectors of the matrix which are not needed.
elements.sent-2411-016: Later solutions improve upon this by calculating the characteristic equation of $\mathbf{K}$ and solving for only the largest eigenvalue ~\cite{QUEST,ESOQ2,FLAE .}
elements.sent-2411-017: For a more comprehensive overview of major algorithms, see~\cite{Wahba_overview .}
elements.sent-2411-018: In recent years, there has been great interest in representing rotations within neural networks which often struggle with learning structured outputs.
elements.sent-2411-019: Directly predicting common parameterizations such as quaternions or Euler angles has generally performed poorly~\cite{learn_SO3 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: In fact, it was shown that any 3D rotation parameterization in less than five real dimensions is discontinuous, necessitating non-minimal representations for smooth learning~\cite{cont_rot .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Additionally, challenges like double cover in some representations can further hinder learning.
elements.sent-2411-022: Two leading approaches~\cite{SVD_analysis,QCQP essentially interpret network outputs as the $\mathbf{B}$ and $\mathbf{K}$ matrices mentioned in the previous section, mapping them to rotations via solutions to Wahba's problem.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Thus, the two tasks can be linked.
elements.sent-2411-025: The mathematical background for special unitary matrices and related concepts is briefly reviewed.

elements.sent-2411-027: The matrix is special unitary if it has the additional restriction that $det(\mathbf{U ) = 1$ exactly.}

elements.sent-2411-320: More formally, it is phrased as seeking the optimal rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}$ that minimizes the following loss: \begin{equation}\min_{R \in SO(3)} \sum_{i} w_{i}||b_{i} - Ra_{i}||^{2} \label{eq:prob}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{a _{i}$ are the reference frame observations, $\mathbf{b _{i}$ are the corresponding target frame observations, and $w_{i}$ are the real positive weights for each observation pair.}}
elements.sent-2411-321: The problem can be solved analytically by finding the nearest special orthogonal matrix (in a Frobenius sense) to the matrix $\mathbf{B}$ below: \begin{equation}B = \sum_{i} w_{i}b_{i}a_{i}^{T} \label{eq:B}\end{equation} Today, this solution is typically computed via singular value decomposition~\cite{Wahba_SVD .\par Alternatively, the solution can be estimated as a unit quaternion.}
elements.sent-2411-322: Davenport introduced the first such method in 1968~\cite{Davenport by showing that the optimal quaternion $\mathbf{q}$ is the eigenvector corresponding to the largest eigenvalue of a 4x4 symmetric gain matrix $\mathbf{K}$, which can be constructed as: \begin{equation}K =  Tr(B) z^T z B + B^T - Tr(B)I  \label{eq:K}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{I}$ is the identity matrix, $Tr(\mathbf{B )=\sum_i\mathbf{B _{ii}$, and $\mathbf{z = \sum_i w_i \mathbf{a _i \times \mathbf{b _i$.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-080: \begin{equation}K =  Tr(B) z^T z B + B^T - Tr(B)I  \label{eq:K}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-323: The solution via eigendecomposition is relatively slow as it solves for all the eigenvectors of the matrix which are not needed.
elements.sent-2411-324: For an overview of major algorithms, see~\cite{Wahba_overview .}
elements.sent-2411-325: Two leading approaches~\cite{SVD_analysis,QCQP essentially interpret network outputs as the $\mathbf{B}$ and $\mathbf{K}$ matrices (\cref{eq:B,eq:K respectively), mapping them to rotations via solutions to Wahba's problem.}}
elements.section-2411-011: Remark on Connection with Hopf Fibration
elements.sent-2411-326: The set of rotations aligning the unit vector $\mathbf{a}$ to $\mathbf{b}$ is described by the kernel of $\mathbf{Q _i$ (\cref{eq:Q ).}}
elements.sent-2411-327: Geometrically, the kernel defines a 2D plane through the origin in 4D space, and its intersection with $S^3$ (representing unit quaternions) forms a great circle.
elements.sent-2411-328: This great circle provides a linear algebra parametrization of an $S^1$ fiber whose image under the Hopf map (for canonical choice of $\mathbf{a}$) is the point $\mathbf{b}$ on $S^2$.
elements.sent-2411-329: A classical description of the fibration is seen in $\cref{sec:degenerate}$ when $\mathbf{u}$ is interpreted as the map's image in $\mathbf{CP ^1$.}
elements.sent-2411-330: The fiber associated with $\mathbf{u}$ is thus parameterized by the scalar $e^{i\theta}$.
